An arithmetic sequence starts at 5 and goes up by 11 each time: 5, 16, 27, 38, ... What is the sum of the first 6 terms?
A sequence starts at 200 and decreases by 17 each time. How many terms are there before the sequence goes below zero?
lessonLearningGoal: How to recognise decreasing sequences where the difference is negative
Why the same method works for both increasing and decreasing

efficacyPairs.questionText: A decreasing sequence starts at 500 and subtracts a different amount each time: 500, 475, 445, 410, 370, ... What is the next term?
lessonLearningGoal: How to recognise decreasing sequences where the difference is negative
Why the same method works for both increasing and decreasing

lessonLearningGoal: How to recognise decreasing sequences where the difference is negative
Why the same method works for both increasing and decreasing

efficacyPairs.questionText: A shop reduces its prices each week. In week 1 an item costs £84, then £72, then £60, then £48. What will it cost in week 6?
lessonLearningGoal: How to recognise decreasing sequences where the difference is negative
Why the same method works for both increasing and decreasing

efficacyPairs.questionText: The rule for a sequence is 'multiply the position number by 5'. What is the 8th term?
lessonLearningGoal: How to write the nth term rule (a formula that finds any term from its position) for an arithmetic sequence (one that goes up or down by the same amount each time)
How to use the rule to find any term (number in the pattern)

efficacyPairs.questionText: A sequence follows the rule '3 times the position number plus 1'. The first few terms are 4, 7, 10, 13, ... What is the 6th term?
lessonLearningGoal: How to write the nth term rule (a formula that finds any term from its position) for an arithmetic sequence (one that goes up or down by the same amount each time)
How to use the rule to find any term (number in the pattern)

efficacyPairs.questionText: The rule for a sequence is '2 times the position number'. Which position gives the term 20?
lessonLearningGoal: How to write the nth term rule (a formula that finds any term from its position) for an arithmetic sequence (one that goes up or down by the same amount each time)
How to use the rule to find any term (number in the pattern)

efficacyPairs.questionText: The square numbers are 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, ... What is the 8th square number?
lessonLearningGoal: How to recognise square, triangular and cube numbers
Why they are called 'special' sequences

efficacyPairs.questionText: The triangular numbers are 1, 3, 6, 10, 15, 21, ... What comes next?
lessonLearningGoal: How to recognise square, triangular and cube numbers
Why they are called 'special' sequences

efficacyPairs.questionText: Which of these is both a square number and a triangular number?
lessonLearningGoal: How to recognise square, triangular and cube numbers
Why they are called 'special' sequences

efficacyPairs.questionText: The cube numbers are 1, 8, 27, 64, ... What is the difference between the 4th and 5th cube numbers?

efficacyPairs.questionText: Each term in a sequence is the sum of the three terms before it: 1, 1, 2, 4, 7, 13, ... What is the next term?
efficacyPairs.subConceptName: Continuing a Sequence Using the Rule
lessonLearningGoal: How to continue a sequence forwards
How to work backwards to find missing earlier terms

efficacyPairs.questionText: A sequence goes: 2, 5, 11, 23, 47, ... Each term is found by doubling and adding 1. What is the next term?
efficacyPairs.subConceptName: Continuing a Sequence Using the Rule
lessonLearningGoal: How to continue a sequence forwards
How to work backwards to find missing earlier terms
